conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess this properly, I'll need to ask you some questions about your circumstances.

First, let me read the official guidance to ensure I give you accurate information.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that. Now I can help you determine your eligibility for Child Benefit.

To start, could you please tell me: **Do you have any children under the age of 16, or children aged 16-19 who are in approved education or training?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I have two children. Alex is 8 and Blake is 14. I'm hoping to find out if I can claim Child Benefit for both of them.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. So you have two children - Alex who is 8 years old and Blake who is 14 years old.

To assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for both Alex and Blake, I need to ask you a few questions.

**Do you currently live in the UK?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I do. I live in Bristol.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great, thank you. That's helpful to know.

**Do both Alex and Blake live with you in your home in Bristol?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, they both live at home with me.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent. Now I need to understand a bit more about your situation.

**Are you responsible for Alex and Blake's day-to-day care? For example, are you the one providing for their food, clothes, and general upkeep?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, absolutely. I'm their parent and I take care of everything for them – food, clothes, school supplies, all of it.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That's very helpful, thank you.

**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for either Alex or Blake?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for them. It's just me looking after them.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect, thank you for clarifying that.

Just a couple more questions to complete the assessment:

**Are either Alex or Blake currently in hospital or care for an extended period, or living away from home for any reason?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, neither of them are in hospital or care. They're both at home with me, healthy and doing well.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That's wonderful to hear. Just one final question:

**Is Blake (your 14-year-old) currently in paid work for 24 hours or more per week, or claiming any benefits in their own right such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Blake doesn't work – well, apart from a bit of pocket money from helping a neighbour with odd jobs now and then, but nothing like that. And no, neither of them receive any benefits in their own name.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent news! **You are eligible to claim Child Benefit for both Alex and Blake.**

Based on the information you've provided:

**For Alex (8 years old):** You meet all the eligibility requirements. Alex is under 16, lives with you in the UK, and you are responsible for their care. No one else is claiming for Alex, and they are not in hospital or care.

**For Blake (14 years old):** You also meet all the eligibility requirements. Blake is under 16, lives with you in the UK, and you are responsible for their care. No one else is claiming for Blake, they are not in hospital or care, and Blake is not working 24+ hours per week or claiming benefits in their own right. The occasional odd jobs for pocket money don't affect eligibility.

You can proceed with making a claim for Child Benefit for both children. Only one person can claim Child Benefit for each child, so as long as no one else claims, you should be able to receive it for both Alex and Blake.

Please note: If you or your partner have an individual income over £60,000, you may need to pay the High Income Child Benefit tax charge, but this doesn't affect your eligibility to claim.

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 8 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 14 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 8 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 14 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
